content.text: Why Use VS Code for Azure Development?

Visual Studio Code, with its Azure extension ecosystem, transforms from a code editor into a lightweight Azure management and deployment console. You can browse Azure resources, deploy web apps, create serverless functions, edit Bicep templates with full IntelliSense, query Cosmos DB databases, and trigger CI/CD pipelines — all from the same window where you write code.

The Azure extensions are built by Microsoft and maintained at the same quality bar as the CLI tools. They connect to Azure using the same Azure AD tokens as the CLI, so there is no separate credential to manage.

attrs.alt: VS Code Azure extension ecosystem: key extensions and their roles

content.text: Getting Started: Installation and Sign-In
Install the Azure Tools extension pack to get the most commonly used extensions in a single install. Or install individual extensions based on what services you use.

# Install Azure Tools extension pack from the command line
code --install-extension ms-vscode.vscode-node-azure-pack

# Or install individual extensions:
code --install-extension ms-azuretools.vscode-azureresourcegroups   # Azure Resources
code --install-extension ms-azuretools.vscode-bicep                  # Bicep
code --install-extension ms-azuretools.vscode-azurefunctions         # Azure Functions
code --install-extension ms-azuretools.vscode-azureappservice        # App Service
code --install-extension ms-azuretools.vscode-cosmosdb              # Azure Databases
code --install-extension ms-azuretools.vscode-azurestorage           # Azure Storage
code --install-extension ms-azuretools.vscode-docker                 # Docker + ACR

After installation, open the Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P / Cmd+Shift+P) and run 'Azure: Sign In'. This opens a browser to authenticate with your Microsoft account. Once signed in, your Azure subscriptions appear in the Azure panel on the left sidebar.
The VS Code Azure extensions use the same OAuth token as az login. If you are already signed into the Azure CLI, VS Code will reuse that session — you may not need to sign in again.
Azure Resources Extension

The Azure Resources extension (ms-azuretools.vscode-azureresourcegroups) is the hub for all Azure extensions. It adds an Azure icon to the activity bar that shows a tree view of your subscriptions, resource groups, and resources.
Browse all resource groups and resources across subscriptions without leaving VS Code.
Right-click any resource to open it in the Azure portal, view its properties, or delete it.
Switch between subscriptions using the subscription filter icon in the Azure panel header.
Use 'Group by' options to view resources by type, location, or tag rather than by resource group.
Open an integrated terminal with the Azure CLI pre-authenticated for the selected subscription.

# VS Code workspace settings to pin subscriptions (settings.json)
# Add to .vscode/settings.json:
# {
#   "azure.tenant": "your-tenant-id",
#   "azureResourceGroups.enableTags": true
# }

# Open the Azure panel: click the Azure icon in the Activity Bar
# Or: View → Open View → Azure

# Sign into a specific tenant (useful for multi-tenant scenarios):
# Command Palette → Azure: Sign In to Azure Cloud
# or: Azure: Sign In with Device Code
Bicep Extension

The Bicep extension (ms-azuretools.vscode-bicep) adds full language support for Azure's native infrastructure-as-code language. It is one of the most useful Azure extensions for anyone working with infrastructure.
Feature
What It Does
IntelliSense
Auto-complete resource types, properties, and parameter names as you type
Type checking
Highlights invalid property values and type mismatches before deployment
Resource snippets
Insert complete resource blocks with Ctrl+Space
Parameter file support
Links .bicepparam files to their .bicep templates
Decompile ARM JSON
Convert existing ARM JSON templates to Bicep with one command
Visualization
Open Bicep Visualizer to see resource dependency graph
Go to definition
Navigate to module definitions and parameter declarations

# Useful Bicep extension commands (Command Palette):
# Bicep: Build Bicep File            → compile to ARM JSON
# Bicep: Decompile into Bicep        → convert ARM JSON → Bicep
# Bicep: Open Bicep Visualizer       → show resource graph
# Bicep: Insert Resource             → scaffold a resource from a live Azure resource

# Deploy directly from VS Code:
# Right-click a .bicep file in the Explorer → Deploy Bicep File
# This prompts for subscription and resource group, then deploys
Azure Functions Extension

The Azure Functions extension (ms-azuretools.vscode-azurefunctions) provides an end-to-end local development experience for serverless functions: create, run, debug, and deploy without leaving VS Code.
Install the Azure Functions Core Tools: npm install -g azure-functions-core-tools@4
Open Command Palette → Azure Functions: Create New Project
Choose a language (C#, JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Java, PowerShell)
Choose a trigger type (HTTP, Timer, Blob, Service Bus, etc.)
VS Code generates the project structure and function stub
Press F5 to start the local Functions runtime and attach the debugger
Set breakpoints in your function code — they hit on local invocations
Right-click the function in the Azure panel → Deploy to Function App
Azure App Service Extension
The Azure App Service extension (ms-azuretools.vscode-azureappservice) lets you deploy and manage web applications, browse log streams, and swap deployment slots directly from the VS Code editor.

# Deploy from VS Code:
# 1. Right-click your project folder in the Explorer
#    → Deploy to Web App
# 2. Select a subscription and App Service (or create a new one)
# 3. VS Code runs the build, packages the app, and deploys via Kudu

# From the Azure panel:
# Expand 'App Services' → right-click your app:
#   Browse Website       → open app in browser
#   View Output Logs     → stream application logs (like az webapp log tail)
#   Restart              → restart the app service
#   Swap Deployment Slots → swap staging ↔ production
#   Open in Portal       → jump to Azure portal

# Configure deployment settings in .vscode/settings.json:
# {
#   "appService.defaultWebAppToDeploy": "/subscriptions/.../resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Web/sites/myapp"
# }
Azure Databases Extension

The Azure Databases extension (ms-azuretools.vscode-cosmosdb) lets you browse and query Cosmos DB, Azure SQL, Azure PostgreSQL, MySQL, and MongoDB databases from a tree view without switching to another tool.
Expand a Cosmos DB account to browse databases, containers, and documents.
Double-click a document to open it in a JSON editor — save to update it in Cosmos DB.
Right-click a container → Open in Scrapbook → write and run NoSQL queries.
For SQL databases: connect with a connection string, then run SQL queries in the editor.
Manage connection strings in the Azure panel; they are stored securely in VS Code's secret store.

// Cosmos DB NoSQL query (run in VS Code Scrapbook)
// The extension sends this to Cosmos DB and shows results inline

SELECT c.id, c.name, c.status, c._ts
FROM c
WHERE c.status = 'active'
AND c._ts > 1700000000
ORDER BY c._ts DESC
OFFSET 0 LIMIT 20
Azure Storage Extension
The Azure Storage extension (ms-azuretools.vscode-azurestorage) provides a tree-view browser for blob containers, file shares, queues, and tables inside Azure Storage accounts.
Browse blob containers: expand any container to see files; double-click to download and open.
Upload files or folders: right-click a container → Upload Files / Upload Folder.
Edit small text blobs directly: open a blob, edit in the VS Code editor, save to upload.
Browse queues: peek, dequeue, and add messages from the tree view.
Browse tables: view and add entities in a grid editor.
Deploy a static website: right-click a storage account → Deploy to Static Website.

# Quick deploy a static site to Azure Storage from VS Code:
# 1. Open your project containing index.html
# 2. Right-click the dist/ folder → Deploy to Static Website
# 3. Select a storage account (must have static website enabled)
# VS Code uploads files and reports the public URL

# Enable static website hosting on a storage account first:
az storage blob service-properties update \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --static-website \
  --index-document index.html \
  --404-document 404.html
Recommended Extension Settings
Add these settings to your VS Code workspace (.vscode/settings.json) or user settings to improve the Azure development experience.
Keyboard Shortcuts and Command Palette Tips
All Azure extension actions are accessible from the Command Palette. Prefixing your search with 'Azure:' or the service name quickly narrows the list.
Command Palette Query
What It Does
Azure: Sign In
Authenticate with Azure AD
Azure: Select Subscriptions
Toggle which subscriptions appear in the panel
Bicep: Open Visualizer
Show resource dependency graph for the open Bicep file
Bicep: Decompile into Bicep
Convert ARM JSON template to Bicep
Azure Functions: Create New Project
Scaffold a new Functions project
Azure Functions: Initialize Project for Use with VS Code
Add VS Code config to existing Functions project
Azure App Service: Deploy to Web App
Deploy current workspace to an App Service
Azure Storage: Deploy to Static Website
Upload static files to a storage account
Azure: Open in Portal
Jump to the selected resource in the Azure portal
Using Dev Containers with Azure Extensions
You can combine VS Code Dev Containers with the Azure extensions to create a reproducible development environment where every team member has the same Azure tools pre-installed.
GitHub Actions Extension for Azure
The GitHub Actions extension (GitHub.vscode-github-actions) integrates CI/CD workflow editing into VS Code. Combined with Azure extensions, it provides a complete pipeline authoring experience.
Syntax highlighting and IntelliSense for .github/workflows/*.yml files.
Auto-complete for action names, inputs, and step properties.
See workflow run status in the VS Code status bar and panel without opening GitHub.
View logs for individual steps directly in VS Code.
Trigger manual workflow runs from the Actions panel.
The Azure Login action (azure/login@v2) integrates with the Azure extensions' credential model.

# Example workflow: deploy to Azure App Service on push to main
# Authored with IntelliSense from the GitHub Actions extension
name: Deploy to Azure App Service

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'

      - run: npm ci && npm run build

      - name: Sign in to Azure
        uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          creds: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CREDENTIALS }}

      - name: Deploy to App Service
        uses: azure/webapps-deploy@v3
        with:
          app-name: myapp
          package: ./dist
Azure Container Apps and Docker Extensions
For containerized workloads, the Docker extension (ms-azuretools.vscode-docker) and Azure Container Apps extension work together to build, push, and deploy container images without leaving VS Code.

# Install Docker and Container Apps extensions
code --install-extension ms-azuretools.vscode-docker
code --install-extension ms-azuretools.vscode-azurecontainerapps

# The Docker extension adds:
# - Tree view of local images, containers, and registries
# - Right-click image → Push to Azure Container Registry
# - Dockerfile IntelliSense and linting
# - docker-compose.yml IntelliSense
# - Connect to Azure Container Registry (ACR) in the Registries panel

# Dockerfile with IntelliSense from the Docker extension
# Extension highlights deprecated instructions, validates COPY paths
FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

FROM node:20-alpine AS runtime
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules
ENV NODE_ENV=production
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]

# Build and push to Azure Container Registry from VS Code terminal
# (or right-click image in Docker panel → Push)
az acr login --name myregistry
docker build -t myregistry.azurecr.io/myapp:latest .
docker push myregistry.azurecr.io/myapp:latest

# Deploy to Container Apps from VS Code:
# Azure panel → Container Apps → right-click app → Update Container App
# or Command Palette → Azure Container Apps: Deploy Container App
Azure Key Vault and Secrets Integration

While there is no standalone Key Vault extension, the Azure Resources extension exposes Key Vault secrets, keys, and certificates in the resource tree. You can also integrate Key Vault secrets into your local development workflow using environment variable injection.

# In the Azure panel, expand a Key Vault resource to:
# - Browse Secrets / Keys / Certificates
# - Right-click a secret → Copy Secret Value (for testing)
# - Right-click → Open in Portal

# For local development: inject Key Vault secrets as environment variables
# .env.local (git-ignored) — fetched from Key Vault at session start:
cat > scripts/fetch-secrets.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
VAULT=mykeyvault

# Export secrets as environment variables
export DB_PASSWORD=$(az keyvault secret show --vault-name $VAULT --name db-password --query value -o tsv)
export API_KEY=$(az keyvault secret show --vault-name $VAULT --name api-key --query value -o tsv)
export STORAGE_CONNSTR=$(az keyvault secret show --vault-name $VAULT --name storage-connstr --query value -o tsv)

echo 'Secrets loaded from Key Vault'
EOF
chmod +x scripts/fetch-secrets.sh
source scripts/fetch-secrets.sh
Debugging Azure Functions Locally

The Azure Functions extension integrates with VS Code's debugger to let you step through function code running locally, inspect variables, set breakpoints, and test triggers before deploying to Azure.

# local.settings.json: local environment variables for Functions
# (never commit this file — it contains secrets)
cat > local.settings.json << 'EOF'
{
  "IsEncrypted": false,
  "Values": {
    "AzureWebJobsStorage": "UseDevelopmentStorage=true",
    "FUNCTIONS_WORKER_RUNTIME": "node",
    "MyApiKey": "local-dev-key",
    "CosmosDbConnection": "AccountEndpoint=https://localhost:8081/;AccountKey=C2y6y..."
  }
}
EOF

# Start debugging: F5 in VS Code
# The Functions runtime starts on http://localhost:7071
# Test the HTTP trigger:
curl http://localhost:7071/api/httpTrigger?name=World
Extension Pack vs Individual Extensions

Microsoft publishes an Azure Tools extension pack that bundles the most commonly used extensions for one-click installation. Understanding what is included helps you decide whether to install the pack or cherry-pick extensions.
Extension
Extension ID
Included in Pack?
Azure Account
ms-vscode.azure-account
Yes
Azure Resources
ms-azuretools.vscode-azureresourcegroups
Yes
Azure App Service
ms-azuretools.vscode-azureappservice
Yes
Azure Functions
ms-azuretools.vscode-azurefunctions
Yes
Azure Storage
ms-azuretools.vscode-azurestorage
Yes
Azure Databases
ms-azuretools.vscode-cosmosdb
Yes
Azure Container Apps
ms-azuretools.vscode-azurecontainerapps
Yes
Bicep
ms-azuretools.vscode-bicep
Yes
Azure Static Web Apps
ms-azuretools.vscode-azurestaticwebapps
Yes
Docker
ms-azuretools.vscode-docker
No — install separately
GitHub Actions
GitHub.vscode-github-actions
No — install separately
Azure Dev CLI (azd)
ms-azuretools.azure-dev
No — install separately

# Install the full Azure Tools extension pack (installs all 'Yes' rows above)
code --install-extension ms-vscode.vscode-node-azure-pack

# List all installed Azure-related extensions
code --list-extensions | grep -i azure

# Disable extensions you don't use to reduce VS Code startup time
# Command Palette → Extensions: Show Installed Extensions → right-click → Disable
Summary

VS Code's Azure extension ecosystem turns the editor into an integrated Azure development environment. The Azure Resources extension provides a subscription-wide resource browser; the Bicep extension adds IntelliSense, type checking, and visualization for infrastructure-as-code files; the Azure Functions extension handles the full local-debug-deploy lifecycle for serverless workloads; and the App Service, Storage, and Databases extensions let you manage and interact with those services without switching to the portal or CLI. All extensions share a single sign-in via the Azure Account extension and can be pre-installed in Dev Containers for reproducible team environments. Use the Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P) prefixed with the service name to discover available actions quickly.
